name: Hyderabadi Chicken Biryani
ingredients: Biryani whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf)
instructions: Marinate chicken with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, half the fried onions, mint, and salt. Leave in the fridge for at least 2 hours.
Wash basmati rice until water runs clear, then soak for 30 minutes.
Boil a large pot of water with whole spices and lots of salt. Add soaked rice and cook until it is 70% done (still has a bite). Drain the water completely.
In a heavy-bottomed pot, spread the marinated chicken in a single layer at the bottom. Layer the hot, partially cooked rice directly over the raw chicken.
Top with remaining fried onions, coriander, and pour the saffron milk over the top. Seal the pot tightly with foil or dough.
Cook on medium-high for 10 minutes, then lower the flame to the absolute lowest setting and cook on 'dum' (steam) for 30 minutes. Rest for 10 minutes before opening.

name: Authentic Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani)
instructions: Marinate chicken chunks in thick yogurt, Kashmiri chili powder, ginger-garlic paste, and lemon juice for 1 hour.
Pan-fry or bake the marinated chicken on high heat until fully cooked and slightly charred. Set aside.
In a deep pan, melt 1 tbsp butter. Sauté roughly chopped onions, tomatoes, and soaked cashews until tomatoes are completely mushy.
Blend the tomato-cashew mixture into a fine, smooth paste. Strain it if you want a restaurant-style silky gravy.
Return the smooth puree to the pan. Add the remaining butter, cooked chicken, and simmer for 10 minutes until oil separates at the edges.
Turn off the heat. Stir in the heavy cream, garam masala, and crush the dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi) between your palms and sprinkle on top.

name: Dhaba Style Palak Paneer
instructions: Boil water in a pot. Drop washed spinach leaves in for exactly 2 minutes (blanching). Immediately remove and submerge in ice-cold water to retain the bright green color.
Blend the cooled spinach and a green chili into a smooth puree without adding too much water.
In a pan, heat oil or ghee. Add cumin seeds and let them crackle. Add minced garlic and sauté until fragrant.
Add finely chopped onions and cook until golden brown. Add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down and release oil.
Pour in the spinach puree, add salt and a pinch of garam masala. Simmer on low heat for 5 minutes (do not cover the pan, or it will lose its green color).
Gently fold in the paneer cubes. Turn off the heat and swirl in the fresh cream before serving.

name: Classic Masala Dosa
ingredients: 3 cups Fermented dosa batter (rice and urad dal)
instructions: Prepare the potato filling (Masala): Heat oil in a pan, add mustard seeds and let them pop. Add curry leaves, green chilies, and sliced onions. Sauté until onions are soft but not brown.
Add turmeric powder and salt to the onions. Roughly mash the boiled potatoes and mix them into the pan. Cook for 2 minutes, garnish with coriander, and set aside.
Heat a cast-iron tawa or non-stick griddle until medium-hot. Sprinkle a few drops of water; if it sizzles and evaporates immediately, it's ready. Wipe the tawa with a clean cloth.
Pour a ladle full of batter in the center of the tawa. Using the back of the ladle, quickly spread it outward in a circular motion to make a thin crepe.
Drizzle 1 tsp of ghee or oil around the edges and center. Roast on medium heat until the bottom turns golden brown and crispy.
Place a portion of the potato masala in the center of the dosa, fold the edges over, and serve hot with chutney and sambar.

name: Punjabi Chole (Chickpea Curry)
instructions: Wash and soak the chickpeas in water overnight (at least 8 hours).
Drain the water. Pressure cook the chickpeas with fresh water, salt, and a black tea bag for 4-5 whistles until they are completely soft and easily mashable. Discard the tea bag.
In a heavy-bottomed pan, heat oil. Add cumin seeds. Add the onion puree and roast patiently on medium heat until it turns a deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and the tomato puree. Cook until the moisture evaporates and oil starts leaving the sides of the masala.
Add the Chole masala powder, red chili powder, and a pinch of turmeric. Roast for 1 minute.
Pour in the boiled chickpeas along with their dark cooking water. Simmer on medium-low heat for 15-20 minutes until the gravy thickens. Garnish with ginger juliennes.

name: Restaurant Style Dal Makhani
instructions: Wash the black lentils and kidney beans rubbing them vigorously. Soak them together in plenty of water overnight.
Pressure cook the soaked lentils and beans with salt and water for 6-7 whistles until they are incredibly soft and mushy.
In a large heavy pot, heat butter. Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute. Add the tomato puree and Kashmiri chili powder.
Cook the tomato mixture until it reduces and releases butter.

Add the boiled lentils with their water. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat to the absolute minimum. Let it simmer uncovered for at least 1 to 2 hours, stirring occasionally and lightly mashing the lentils against the side of the pot.
Once the dal reaches a creamy consistency, stir in the heavy cream and a pinch of garam masala. Serve hot.

name: Paneer Tikka Masala
instructions: In a bowl, mix yogurt, roasted besan, ginger-garlic paste, a splash of mustard oil, and all the tikka spices. Add the paneer, bell pepper, and onion squares. Coat well and marinate for 30 minutes.
Skewer the marinated items or place them directly on a hot grill pan. Cook until all sides have dark, charred spots. Set aside.
In a separate pan, heat oil. Finely chop an onion and sauté until golden brown.
Add tomato puree and cook until the oil separates from the masala.
Add a splash of water and the cream to create a rich gravy. Gently drop in the grilled paneer and veggies.
Simmer for 5 minutes, garnish with fresh coriander, and serve.

name: Kashmiri Rogan Josh
instructions: Heat mustard oil in a heavy-bottomed pot until it smokes. Turn off the heat, let it cool slightly, then turn it back on to medium. Add the whole spices.
Add the mutton pieces. Fry them patiently for 10-15 minutes until they are well browned and sealed on all sides.
In a small bowl, mix the whisked yogurt with Kashmiri chili powder, fennel powder, and dry ginger powder. Lower the flame completely and add this yogurt mix to the meat.
Stir vigorously to prevent the yogurt from curdling. Cook until the yogurt blends in and the oil separates.
Add 2 cups of hot water. Cover the pot tightly and slow-cook on low heat for 60 to 75 minutes, or until the meat is incredibly tender and falling off the bone.

name: Homestyle Dal Tadka (Yellow Lentil)
instructions: Wash the toor dal thoroughly. Pressure cook it with 3 cups of water, chopped tomato, turmeric, and a pinch of salt for 3-4 whistles.
Let the pressure release naturally. Open and whisk the dal slightly to achieve a smooth consistency. Add warm water if it's too thick.
Prepare the Tadka (Tempering): Heat ghee in a small pan. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter.
Add the minced garlic and sauté until it starts turning golden brown. Add the dried red chilies.
Turn off the heat, immediately add a pinch of red chili powder to the hot ghee, and immediately pour the sizzling tadka over the cooked dal.
Cover the pot for 2 minutes to trap the aromas. Stir and serve hot with rice.

name: Malai Kofta
instructions: Make the Koftas: Combine mashed potatoes, grated paneer, cornflour, and salt. Knead into a smooth dough. Take a small portion, flatten it, place a cashew and raisin in the center, and roll it into a smooth, crack-free ball.
Heat oil in a wok. Deep fry the kofta balls on medium heat until golden brown. Drain on paper towels.
Make the Gravy: In a pan, sauté roughly chopped onions, tomatoes, and cashews with whole spices until completely soft. Let it cool, then blend into a very smooth paste.
Heat a little oil in a pan, strain the blended paste into it (for a silky texture). Cook for 5 minutes.
Add water, salt, a pinch of sugar, and garam masala. Bring to a boil.
Turn off the heat, stir in the cream. Place the fried koftas in a serving dish and pour the hot gravy over them just before serving so they don't break.

name: Aloo Gobi (Potato & Cauliflower)
instructions: Wash and completely dry the cauliflower florets and potato cubes.
In a wide pan, heat 2 tbsp oil. Pan-fry the potatoes for 5 minutes until edges are golden. Add the cauliflower and fry for another 5 minutes. Remove veggies from the pan and set aside.
In the same pan, add a little more oil. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Sauté the chopped onions and ginger until onions are translucent.
Add chopped tomatoes, turmeric, coriander powder, and salt. Cook until tomatoes are mushy.
Return the fried potatoes and cauliflower to the pan. Toss well to coat them in the masala.
Cover the pan with a tight lid, lower the heat, and let it cook in its own steam for 10-12 minutes until veggies are tender. Do not add water.

name: Bhindi Masala (Spiced Okra)
instructions: Crucial step: Wash the okra and wipe each pod completely dry with a kitchen towel before chopping. This prevents sliminess. Chop off the ends and cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat 1 tbsp oil in a wide pan. Add the chopped okra and fry on medium-high heat for 8-10 minutes until the sliminess is gone and they shrink slightly. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, heat remaining oil. Add cumin seeds, then add the sliced onions. Sauté until lightly browned.
Add the chopped tomatoes, turmeric, coriander powder, and salt. Cook until tomatoes are soft.
Add the fried okra back to the pan. Mix gently. Cook uncovered for 3-4 minutes.
Turn off the heat and sprinkle the amchur (mango powder) over the top and mix well. Serve hot with roti.

name: Rajma Chawal (Kidney Bean Curry)
ingredients: 1 tbsp Rajma Masala or Garam masala
instructions: Soak the kidney beans overnight. Drain and pressure cook with fresh water and salt for 5-6 whistles until completely soft.
Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot. Add cumin seeds. Sauté the grated onions patiently until they turn deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute. Add the tomato puree, turmeric, red chili powder, and Rajma masala.
Cook the masala until the oil separates from the sides (this bhuna process is key to good rajma).
Add the boiled kidney beans along with their cooking water. Bring to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer for 15-20 minutes to let the flavors meld.
Lightly mash a few beans against the side of the pot to thicken the gravy. Serve hot over steamed Basmati rice.

name: Kanda Poha
instructions: Place the poha in a colander and gently rinse it under running water for 10 seconds. Do not soak it. Sprinkle a little salt and sugar over the wet poha and let it sit.
Heat oil in a pan. Fry the peanuts until crunchy, remove and set aside.
In the same oil, add mustard seeds and let them pop. Add curry leaves and green chilies.
Add the tiny potato cubes. Cover and cook for 3-4 minutes until the potatoes are soft.
Add chopped onions and sauté until translucent. Stir in the turmeric powder.
Add the softened poha and roasted peanuts to the pan. Toss gently so the poha doesn't break. Cover and steam on low heat for 2 minutes. Finish with a generous squeeze of lemon juice.

name: Homestyle Chicken Curry
instructions: Heat 3 tbsp oil in a heavy-bottomed pan. Add the whole spices and let them release their aroma.
Add the finely chopped onions. Sauté patiently on medium heat until they turn dark brown. This creates the base of the curry.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 1 minute. Add the chicken pieces and fry them with the onions for 5-7 minutes until the chicken turns white.
Add the turmeric, coriander powder, red chili powder, and salt. Toss well to coat the chicken.
Pour in the tomato puree. Cook for another 5 minutes until the tomatoes break down.
Add 1.5 cups of hot water. Cover and simmer on medium-low heat for 20 minutes until the chicken is tender. Garnish with fresh coriander.

name: Paneer Bhurji (Scrambled Paneer)
ingredients: 1/2 tsp Pav Bhaji Masala or Garam masala
instructions: Heat a little oil or butter in a pan. Add cumin seeds and let them crackle.
Add finely chopped onions and sauté until they turn translucent. Add green chilies and ginger.
Add chopped capsicum and sauté for 2 minutes. Then add chopped tomatoes, turmeric, and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes are soft and mushy. Add the Pav Bhaji masala (or garam masala) and mix well.
Add the crumbled paneer. Toss everything together and cook for just 2-3 minutes. Do not overcook, or the paneer will become rubbery.
Garnish generously with fresh coriander and serve hot with parathas or bread.

name: Aloo Paratha (Stuffed Potato Bread)
instructions: Knead the whole wheat flour with water and a pinch of salt into a soft, pliable dough. Cover and let it rest for 15 minutes.
Peel and thoroughly mash the boiled potatoes. Make sure there are no lumps. Mix in the finely chopped onion, chilies, ajwain, amchur, salt, and fresh coriander.
Take a golf-ball sized portion of dough. Roll it into a small 3-inch circle.
Place a generous spoonful of the potato mixture in the center. Bring the edges of the dough together and pinch to seal the filling inside.
Dust with dry flour and gently roll it out into a flat circle. Use light pressure so the filling doesn't burst out.
Place the paratha on a hot tawa (griddle). Cook until small bubbles appear, flip, spread ghee or butter, and press with a spatula until golden brown spots form on both sides.

name: Egg Curry (Anda Masala)
instructions: Make shallow, vertical slits on the boiled eggs. Heat a drop of oil in a pan, sprinkle a pinch of turmeric and red chili powder, and pan-fry the eggs for 2 minutes until lightly golden. Set aside.
In the same pan, heat the remaining oil. Add cumin seeds, then the finely chopped onions. Sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add the tomato puree, salt, and dry spices (coriander powder, garam masala). Cook until the oil separates from the masala.
Add 1 cup of water to make a gravy and bring to a simmer. Drop the fried eggs into the gravy.
Cover and cook on low heat for 5 minutes so the eggs absorb the flavors. Serve with roti or rice.

name: Comforting Moong Dal Khichdi
ingredients: 1/2 cup Yellow Moong Dal (split green gram)
instructions: Wash the rice and moong dal together until the water runs clear. Soak for 15 minutes.
In a pressure cooker, heat the ghee. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Add the hing (asafoetida).
If using vegetables, add the chopped veggies and sauté for a minute.
Drain the rice and dal and add them to the cooker. Add turmeric and salt.
Add 3.5 to 4 cups of water (Khichdi needs a lot of water to become soft and porridge-like).
Close the lid and pressure cook for 4-5 whistles on medium heat. Let pressure release naturally. Serve hot with a dollop of extra ghee and pickle.

name: Jeera Aloo (Cumin Potatoes)
instructions: Ensure your boiled potatoes are completely cool before cubing them. If they are hot, they will turn to mush when cooking.
Heat oil or ghee in a pan on medium heat. It must be hot enough so the cumin seeds crackle immediately.
Add the cumin seeds (be generous, it's the star ingredient) and let them brown slightly. Add green chilies.
Lower the heat. Add turmeric, chili powder, and immediately drop in the potato cubes. Toss well to coat the potatoes in the spiced oil.
Cook uncovered for 5-6 minutes, tossing occasionally, until the potatoes get a slight crispy crust.
Turn off the heat. Sprinkle amchur powder, salt, and a large handful of chopped fresh coriander. Mix and serve.

name: Chana Masala
instructions: Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds. Sauté the chopped onions until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, and red chili powder. Sauté for a few seconds.
Pour in the tomato puree. Cook the mixture until the oil separates from the edges.
Add the Chana Masala powder and salt. Mix well.
Add the boiled chickpeas along with half a cup of water. Simmer on medium-low heat for 10-15 minutes.
Finish with a sprinkle of garam masala and fresh coriander. Serve with Puri, Bhature, or Rice.

name: South Indian Lemon Rice
instructions: Make sure the cooked rice is completely cool and the grains are separate.
Heat oil in a pan. Add the peanuts and fry until crunchy. Push them to the side.
Add mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add the chana dal, split urad dal, dried red chilies, and curry leaves. Sauté until the dals turn golden.
Turn off the heat. Add the turmeric powder (adding it off the heat prevents it from burning and turning brown).
Immediately add the lemon juice and salt to the pan, then add the cooked rice.
Gently mix everything together until the rice takes on a beautiful yellow color. Serve warm or at room temperature.

name: Upma (Savory Semolina Breakfast)
instructions: Dry roast the semolina in a pan on low heat until it smells nutty (about 5 mins). Do not let it turn brown. Remove to a plate.
In the same pan, heat oil or ghee. Add mustard seeds, urad dal, curry leaves, and green chilies.
Add the chopped onions and sauté until translucent. Add the green peas and carrots if using. Sauté for a minute.
Pour exactly 2.5 cups of boiling hot water into the pan. Add salt.
Lower the heat to minimum. Slowly pour the roasted semolina into the boiling water with one hand while continuously stirring with the other to prevent lumps.
Cover with a lid and let it steam for 3 minutes. Turn off the heat, drizzle a teaspoon of ghee on top, and fluff with a fork.

name: Mutter Paneer (Peas & Cottage Cheese)
instructions: In a pan, heat oil and pan-fry the paneer cubes lightly until the edges are golden. Soak them in warm water to keep them soft.
In the same pan, add cumin seeds, followed by the onion puree. Sauté until lightly browned.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for 1 minute, then add tomato puree, turmeric, and coriander powder.
Cook until the oil separates. Stir in the cashew paste and cook for 2 more minutes.
Add the green peas and half a cup of water. Simmer until the peas are tender.
Drain the paneer cubes and add them to the gravy along with garam masala. Simmer for 3-4 minutes. Serve hot.

name: Gajar Ka Halwa (Carrot Pudding)
instructions: In a heavy-bottomed pot or kadhai, combine the grated carrots and the full-fat milk.
Bring to a boil, then lower the heat. Cook uncovered, stirring frequently, until the milk is completely absorbed by the carrots. This takes about 40-45 minutes.
Once the milk has evaporated, add the sugar. The mixture will become watery again. Continue to cook until this liquid also evaporates.
Add the ghee. Sauté the halwa in the ghee for 10 minutes until it turns a deep, rich red color and smells roasted.
Turn off the heat. Stir in the cardamom powder and roasted nuts. Serve warm.

name: Baingan Bharta (Roasted Eggplant Mash)
instructions: Wash the eggplant and dry it. Make small slits in it and stuff the garlic cloves inside. Rub a few drops of mustard oil over the skin.
Roast the eggplant directly over an open gas flame, turning occasionally, until the skin is completely charred and the inside is soft and mushy (about 10-15 mins).
Let it cool, peel off the charred skin, and mash the flesh thoroughly with a fork.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds, ginger, and green chilies. Sauté the chopped onions until golden brown.
Add the chopped tomatoes, turmeric, coriander powder, and salt. Cook until the tomatoes are mushy.
Add the mashed eggplant. Mix well and cook for 5-7 minutes so the smoky flavor infuses into the masala. Garnish with coriander.

name: Punjabi Kadhi Pakora
instructions: Make the Pakoras: Mix 1 cup besan, sliced onions, green chilies, a pinch of baking soda, and water to make a thick batter. Deep fry spoonfuls in hot oil until golden. Set aside.
Make the Kadhi: Whisk the sour yogurt and 1/2 cup besan together until completely smooth. Add 4 cups of water, turmeric, and salt. Whisk again.
In a deep pot, heat oil. Add fenugreek seeds, mustard seeds, and dried red chilies. Pour the yogurt-besan mixture into the pot.
Bring it to a boil while stirring continuously (if you stop stirring, the yogurt will curdle).
Once it boils, lower the heat and let it simmer for 30 minutes until it thickens.
Drop the fried pakoras into the simmering kadhi. Boil for 5 more minutes. Serve with steamed rice.

name: Aloo Matar (Potato & Peas Curry)
instructions: Heat oil in a pressure cooker or heavy pan. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter.
Add the onion puree and sauté until it turns light brown. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add the tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chili powder. Cook until oil separates from the masala.
Toss in the potato cubes and green peas. Mix well to coat them in the spices.
Add 1.5 cups of water and salt. Pressure cook for 2 whistles (or cover and simmer for 15 minutes until potatoes are soft).
Sprinkle garam masala and fresh coriander before serving.

name: Lobiya Masala (Black-Eyed Peas)
instructions: Soak the black-eyed peas in hot water for 2 hours (or overnight). Drain and pressure cook with fresh water and salt for 3-4 whistles until tender.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds, and sauté the chopped onions until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, then pour in the tomato puree along with turmeric, coriander, and chili powder.
Cook the masala until it thickens and leaves oil at the sides.
Add the boiled lobiya along with its cooking water. Simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes so the flavors blend.
Garnish with garam masala and coriander. Serve with roti or rice.

name: Methi Malai Matar
ingredients: 10 Cashews (soaked and blended into a paste)
instructions: Sprinkle a little salt on the chopped methi leaves and let them sit for 10 mins. Squeeze out the bitter water, then briefly sauté the leaves in 1 tsp oil. Set aside.
Boil the green peas until tender.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds and onion puree. Sauté until translucent. Add ginger-garlic paste.
Add a splash of water, turmeric, and coriander powder. Sauté for a minute.
Lower the heat and stir in the cashew paste and heavy cream. Keep stirring so it doesn't curdle.
Add the boiled peas and sautéed methi leaves. Simmer for 5 minutes. Do not boil vigorously. Serve hot.

name: Kerala Fish Curry (Meen Curry)
ingredients: 500g Firm Fish (Seer fish or Kingfish, sliced)
instructions: Soak the kokum in warm water for 15 minutes.
Heat coconut oil in an earthen pot (or heavy pan). Add mustard seeds and let them pop. Add curry leaves, green chilies, and sliced onions. Sauté until soft.
Lower the heat. Add turmeric, coriander powder, and a generous amount of Kashmiri red chili powder. Sauté for 30 seconds.
Add the soaked kokum along with its water and 1 cup of plain water. Bring to a rapid boil.
Gently slide in the fish pieces. Lower the heat, cover, and cook for 10-12 minutes until the fish is opaque and cooked through.
Stir in the thick coconut milk, simmer for 2 minutes (do not boil), and remove from heat.

name: Chicken Chettinad
instructions: Make the Chettinad Masala: Dry roast the grated coconut, coriander seeds, dried chilies, peppercorns, fennel seeds, and a cinnamon stick until fragrant and golden. Cool and blend into a smooth paste with a little water.
Heat oil in a pan, add curry leaves and finely chopped onions. Sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and chopped tomatoes. Cook until mushy.
Add the chicken pieces and fry for 5 minutes until they change color.
Pour in the blended Chettinad masala paste and salt. Toss well.
Add 1 cup of water, cover, and simmer for 25 minutes until the chicken is tender and the oil floats to the top.

name: Homestyle Mutton Curry
ingredients: 1 tbsp Meat Masala or Garam Masala
instructions: Heat mustard oil in a pressure cooker until it smokes. Lower heat, add whole spices (bay leaf, cloves, cardamom).
Add the chopped onions and sauté patiently until dark brown.
Add the mutton pieces. Turn the heat up and fry the meat for 10-15 minutes until it is browned all over.
Add ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chili powder. Sauté for 5 mins.
Add the tomatoes and cook until they melt into the gravy.
Add 2 cups of hot water and salt. Close the pressure cooker and cook for 5-6 whistles on medium-low heat. Let pressure release naturally.

name: Prawn Masala (Shrimp Curry)
instructions: Marinate the cleaned prawns with a pinch of turmeric, salt, and half a lemon's juice for 10 mins.
Heat coconut oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chilies.
Sauté the chopped onions until light brown. Add ginger-garlic paste.
Add tomatoes, coriander powder, and Kashmiri red chili powder. Cook until oil separates.
Add the marinated prawns and sauté for exactly 3-4 minutes (prawns cook very fast and turn rubbery if overcooked).
Pour in the coconut milk, simmer for 2 minutes, and turn off the heat. Serve with rice or appam.

name: Vegetable Pulao
ingredients: 1 cup Mixed Vegetables (carrots, beans, peas, potato)
instructions: Wash and soak the basmati rice for 30 minutes. Drain completely.
In a heavy pot or pressure cooker, heat ghee. Add the whole spices and let them crackle.
Add sliced onions and sauté until translucent (don't let them turn brown).
Add the chopped mixed vegetables, a handful of mint leaves, and sauté for 2 minutes.
Add the drained rice and very gently stir to coat the grains in ghee.
Pour exactly 2.5 cups of water and salt. Bring to a boil, then lower the heat to minimum, cover tightly, and cook for 12 minutes. Fluff gently with a fork.

name: Tamarind Rice (Puliyogare)
instructions: Spread the cooked rice on a large plate, drizzle 1 tsp sesame oil over it, and let it cool completely.
Heat sesame oil in a pan. Fry peanuts until crunchy and set aside.
In the same oil, add mustard seeds, chana dal, urad dal, dried red chilies, and curry leaves. Sauté until golden.
Lower the heat and pour in the thick tamarind extract. Add turmeric, salt, and the Puliyogare/Rasam powder.
Boil the mixture until it thickens into a dark, oily paste (Gojju).
Turn off the heat. Add the cooked rice and roasted peanuts. Mix thoroughly but gently until the rice is evenly coated.

name: Ven Pongal (Savory Lentil Rice)
instructions: Dry roast the moong dal on low heat for 3 minutes until fragrant. Wash the dal and rice together.
Pressure cook the rice and dal with 3.5 cups of water and salt for 4-5 whistles until extremely soft and mushy.
Open the cooker and mash the mixture well with the back of a spoon.
Prepare the Tempering: Heat ghee in a pan. Add whole black peppercorns, cumin seeds, chopped ginger, and curry leaves. Sauté for 30 seconds.
Add cashews and fry until golden.
Pour this sizzling ghee tempering over the mashed pongal, mix well, and serve hot with coconut chutney.

name: Idli & Sambar
instructions: Make Idlis: Grease idli plates, pour the batter, and steam for 10-12 minutes on medium-high heat. Let cool slightly before unmolding.
Make Sambar: Pressure cook the toor dal until mushy. In a separate pot, boil the chopped veggies in water with turmeric and salt until tender.
Extract the tamarind juice and add it to the boiled veggies. Add the sambar powder and boil for 5 minutes until the raw smell disappears.
Mash the cooked dal and pour it into the boiling veggie mixture. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Heat oil in a small pan, crackle mustard seeds, dried chilies, and curry leaves. Pour this tadka over the hot sambar.

name: Medu Vada (Crispy Lentil Donuts)
ingredients: 1 cup Whole white Urad dal (soaked for 4 hours)
instructions: Drain the soaked urad dal completely. Grind it in a blender or wet grinder using as little ice-cold water as possible until it forms a thick, fluffy batter.
Vigorously whisk the batter with your hand for 5 minutes to incorporate air. (Test: Drop a bit of batter in water; it should float).
Mix in the chopped ginger, chilies, peppercorns, curry leaves, and salt.
Heat oil in a deep kadhai. Wet your hands. Take a lemon-sized ball of batter, flatten it slightly on your fingers, make a hole in the center with your thumb.
Gently slide the vada into the hot oil. Fry on medium heat, turning occasionally, until crispy and golden brown on both sides.
Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar.

name: Mumbai Pav Bhaji
ingredients: 1 cup Mixed veggies (Cauliflower, Peas, Carrots - boiled)
instructions: Mash the boiled potatoes and boiled mixed vegetables thoroughly.
Heat 2 tbsp butter and a little oil in a large flat pan or tawa. Add chopped onions and sauté until translucent.
Add ginger-garlic paste and chopped capsicum. Sauté for 3 minutes.
Add chopped tomatoes and cook until they are completely mushy. Use a potato masher to mash the onion-tomato mixture directly in the pan.
Add the Pav Bhaji masala, Kashmiri red chili powder, and salt. Cook for 1 minute.
Add the mashed boiled veggies and a splash of water. Mash everything together vigorously on the pan for 5-7 minutes. Finish with a dollop of butter and fresh coriander.
Slit the pav bread, apply butter, and toast it on a hot pan until crisp. Serve hot.

name: Misal Pav
ingredients: 2 tbsp Misal Masala or Goda Masala
instructions: Boil the sprouted moth beans with turmeric and salt until tender but not mushy.
Heat a generous amount of oil in a pot (Misal is known for its oily, spicy 'Tarri' or layer of oil on top). Add mustard seeds and curry leaves.
Sauté the chopped onions until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and tomatoes. Cook until oil separates.
Add the Misal masala, red chili powder, and sauté well.
Add the boiled sprouts along with 3-4 cups of water. Boil vigorously for 10-15 minutes until the spicy oil floats to the top.
To serve: In a bowl, add the sprouts, pour the spicy gravy over it, top generously with raw chopped onions, coriander, and crispy Farsan. Serve with buttered Pav.

name: Vada Pav
instructions: Heat 1 tbsp oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds, let them crackle, add curry leaves and the ginger-garlic-chili paste. Sauté for a minute.
Add turmeric, then add the mashed potatoes and salt. Mix well, turn off the heat, and let it cool. Roll into golf-sized balls.
Make a thick batter with besan, a pinch of turmeric, baking soda, salt, and water.
Heat oil for deep frying. Dip the potato balls into the besan batter to coat evenly, and drop them into the hot oil.
Fry until golden and crispy. Remove and drain on paper towels.
Slit a Pav in half. Spread dry garlic chutney (and green chutney if preferred) inside. Place the hot Batata Vada inside and press gently. Serve with fried green chilies.

name: Gujarati Khaman Dhokla
instructions: Sift the besan. In a bowl, mix besan, semolina, lemon juice, sugar, salt, and water to make a smooth, lump-free batter. Let it rest for 15 minutes.
Prepare a steamer and grease a steaming pan.
Just before steaming, add the Eno fruit salt to the batter and whisk gently in one direction. The batter will become very frothy.
Immediately pour the batter into the greased pan and steam on high heat for 15-20 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.
Let it cool slightly, then cut into squares.
Prepare the Tempering: Heat oil, add mustard seeds, slit green chilies, and curry leaves. Add 1/2 cup of water, 1 tbsp sugar, and a squeeze of lemon. Boil for a minute.
Pour this sweet and sour tempering water evenly over the steamed Dhokla so it stays moist. Garnish with coriander and grated coconut.

name: Methi Thepla
ingredients: 1 cup Fresh fenugreek leaves (Methi, washed and chopped)
instructions: In a large bowl, mix the wheat flour, besan, chopped methi leaves, yogurt, ginger-chili paste, turmeric, ajwain, salt, and 1 tbsp oil.
Knead into a soft, smooth dough. The moisture from the methi and yogurt means you will need very little, if any, water.
Divide the dough into small, equal-sized balls.
Dust a ball with dry flour and roll it out into a thin circle (thinner than a paratha).
Heat a tawa (griddle). Place the rolled thepla on it. When small bubbles appear, flip it.
Smear a little oil on both sides and press gently with a spatula until light brown spots appear. They cook very fast. Stack them and serve with yogurt and pickle.

name: Aloo Methi (Potatoes & Fenugreek)
instructions: Pluck the methi leaves from the stems. Wash them thoroughly in multiple changes of water. Drain completely and chop roughly.
Heat mustard oil in a heavy pan until it smokes. Turn off the heat, let it cool slightly, and turn it back on.
Add dried red chilies and crushed garlic. Sauté for 10 seconds.
Add the potato cubes and turmeric. Pan-fry the potatoes uncovered on medium heat for 10-12 minutes until they are 80% cooked and have a golden crust.
Add the chopped methi leaves and salt. The leaves will wilt immediately.
Mix well and cook uncovered for another 5-7 minutes until the potatoes are completely tender and the moisture from the leaves has evaporated.

name: Fish Amritsari (Fried Fish)
ingredients: 400g White fish fillets (Tilapia or Basa, cut into fingers)
instructions: First Marinade: Rub the fish pieces with lemon juice, ginger-garlic paste, and a little salt. Set aside for 15 minutes.
Second Marinade: In a bowl, mix besan, ajwain, red chili powder, turmeric, and a little water to make a thick, coating batter.
Dip the marinated fish pieces into the besan batter, ensuring they are well coated.
Heat oil in a kadhai on medium-high heat. Carefully drop the fish pieces into the oil.
Fry until golden brown and crispy on the outside, and cooked through on the inside (about 4-5 minutes).
Drain on paper towels. Sprinkle generously with Chaat Masala and serve hot with green chutney.

name: Peshawari Chole (Dry Chickpeas)
instructions: Unlike Punjabi Chole, Peshawari Chole is semi-dry and heavily spiced with Anardana (pomegranate seed powder) for tartness.
Heat oil in a pan. Sauté chopped onions until deep brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and the tomato puree. Cook until oil separates.
Add the dry spices: Coriander powder, cumin powder, garam masala, red chili powder, and the crucial Anardana powder.
Add the boiled chickpeas with just a tiny splash of their cooking water.
Cook on medium heat until the masala coats the chickpeas completely and the dish is semi-dry. Garnish with ginger juliennes and slit green chilies.

name: Chicken Sukka (Mangalorean Style)
instructions: Dry roast the coriander seeds, dried chilies, fennel, and whole spices. Then add 3/4 cup of the grated coconut and roast until golden brown. Grind this into a coarse paste with a little water and tamarind.
Heat coconut oil in a pan. Sauté sliced onions and curry leaves until golden.
Add the chicken pieces, turmeric, and salt. Cook for 10 minutes in its own juices.
Add the ground coconut-spice paste to the chicken. Mix well.
Cover and cook until the chicken is completely tender.
Uncover, add the remaining 1/4 cup of fresh grated coconut, and dry roast the mixture in the pan until the gravy completely dries up and coats the chicken pieces.

name: Bisi Bele Bath (Hot Lentil Rice)
ingredients: 1 cup Mixed veggies (carrots, beans, peas, potatoes)
instructions: Pressure cook the rice, toor dal, and mixed vegetables together with 4 cups of water and a pinch of turmeric until extremely soft and mushy.
In a large heavy-bottomed pot, extract the tamarind juice and boil it with the Bisi Bele Bath powder, salt, and a small piece of jaggery until the raw smell is gone.
Pour the mashed rice-dal-veggie mixture into the boiling tamarind water. Mix thoroughly. It should have a porridge-like consistency (add hot water if too thick).
Simmer for 5 minutes.
Prepare Tadka: Heat ghee, fry cashews, mustard seeds, and curry leaves. Pour over the hot Bisi Bele Bath. Serve with potato chips or boondi.

name: Shahi Paneer
ingredients: Pinch of Saffron soaked in milk
instructions: Boil roughly chopped onions in water for 10 mins. Drain and blend into a smooth white paste. (This makes a white/yellow gravy instead of a red tomato one).
Heat oil and butter in a pan. Add whole spices (green cardamom, cloves, cinnamon).
Add the boiled onion paste. Sauté carefully (it splutters) until it turns slightly pinkish but not brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Lower the heat completely. Slowly whisk in the yogurt and cashew-almond paste. Keep stirring to prevent curdling.
Cook until oil shows on the surface. Add salt, white pepper powder, and cardamom powder.
Add paneer triangles, cream, and saffron milk. Simmer gently for 3-4 minutes. Serve hot with Naan.
